title: Yorkshire parkin
subtitle: Traditional oat and treacle cake that improves over a week
excerpt: A dense, dark Yorkshire parkin made with oatmeal, black treacle, golden syrup, and ground ginger. Wrap it and leave it for three to five days before eating — the texture transforms from crumbly to dense and sticky as the treacle works through the oats. A Bonfire Night standard.
sourceNotes: Yorkshire parkin is a documented British regional bake associated with the North of England and Bonfire Night. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): documents parkin as a Yorkshire and Lancashire tradition, specifying rolled oats and black treacle as the distinguishing ingredients and noting the importance of maturing the cake for several days before eating. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the gingerbread chapter includes a parkin recipe with oatmeal, treacle, and ground ginger, baked in a flat tin. The requirement to mature parkin before eating is documented across nineteenth-century household manuals as essential to the final texture.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze after the maturing period. Cut into squares, wrap individually, and freeze. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Parkin must be made at least 3 days before eating, and is best after 5–7 days. Wrap tightly in greaseproof paper and store in a tin at room temperature.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. Very low heat — parkin burns easily at the edges if the oven is too hot. The centre will seem underset when it comes out; it firms as it cools

glossaryTerms.definition: The deliberate maturing process for parkin: the freshly baked cake is wrapped in greaseproof paper and stored for 3–7 days before eating. Over this period, the oats absorb moisture from the treacle and the texture transforms from crumbly to dense, sticky, and deeply flavoured. Parkin eaten within a day of baking is considered underripe and will taste dry and grainy.

content.text: Parkin is unlike most cakes: you bake it, wrap it, and put it away. Do not eat it. Leave it in a tin for at least three days. The oats are hard and dry when the cake comes out of the oven, but over the following days the treacle and golden syrup work through the oat structure, softening and binding it into the dense, dark, sticky cake that parkin is supposed to be. Fresh parkin is an unfinished product.

items.prepNote: medium oatmeal is traditional but rolled oats work well

content.text: Preheat the oven to 150°C fan. Butter and line the square tin. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{black-treacle}}, {{golden-syrup}}, and {{dark-brown-sugar}} together in a small saucepan over low heat, stirring until smooth. Do not let it boil. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{whole-milk}} and beaten {{eggs}}.

In a large bowl, combine the {{rolled-oats}}, {{plain-flour}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{mixed-spice}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Pour the warm treacle mixture over the dry ingredients and stir to combine. The batter will be loose and dark.

Pour into the tin. Bake for 42–45 minutes. The top will feel just firm when pressed and a skewer will come out with a few moist crumbs attached. The cake will look slightly underset but will firm as it cools. Leave in the tin until completely cold, then turn out, wrap tightly in greaseproof paper, and store in a tin. This is the start of the
. Do not eat it for at least 3 days. Cut into squares when ready.
